g.data-narration: Welcome to our Text Search Algorithms module. We'll explore efficient pattern matching techniques used in search engines and bioinformatics applications.
g.text: Text Search Algorithms

g.data-narration: Text search algorithms find patterns within large text data. They power search engines, plagiarism detectors, and DNA sequence matching applications.
g.text: What is Text Search?
Text search algorithms locate pattern strings within larger text strings.
Key algorithms: Naive, KMP, Boyer-Moore, Rabin-Karp, Finite Automata.
Applications: Search engines, plagiarism detection, DNA sequencing, network security.

g.data-narration: The naive approach compares the pattern at every text position sequentially. It has O(n × m) time complexity but is simple to implement.
g.text: Naive String Matching
Compares pattern at every position (0 to n-m). Time: O(n×m), Space: O(1).
Example: Pattern "aba" in "ababaaba" → matches at positions 0, 2, 5.
Drawback: Performs redundant comparisons after mismatches.

g.data-narration: KMP achieves O(n + m) time by using a precomputed prefix function. When a mismatch occurs, it skips redundant character comparisons.
g.text: Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP)
Uses prefix function (lps array) - longest proper prefix that is also a suffix.
Time: O(n+m), Space: O(m). Preprocessing computes lps in O(m).
Example: Pattern "ABABAC" → lps = [0, 0, 1, 2, 3, 0]

g.data-narration: Boyer-Moore scans the pattern from right to left. It uses bad character and good suffix rules to skip large text sections efficiently.
g.text: Boyer-Moore Algorithm
Scans pattern right-to-left (opposite of most algorithms). Two heuristics:
Bad Character Rule: Align mismatched text char with its rightmost pattern occurrence.
Good Suffix Rule: Shift using matched suffix information. Avg: O(n/m), Worst: O(n×m).

g.data-narration: Rabin-Karp uses hashing for fast matching with rolling hash. Finite automata guarantee O(n) search time after O(m × σ) preprocessing.
g.text: Rabin-Karp & Finite Automata
Rabin-Karp: Uses rolling hash for O(1) substring hash computation. Avg O(n+m).
Finite Automata: Builds DFA with (m+1) states. Search O(n), Preprocess O(m×σ).
σ = alphabet size. Drawback: Large memory for transition table.

g.data-narration: Choose naive for short patterns, KMP for consistent linear performance, Boyer-Moore for large alphabets, Rabin-Karp for multiple pattern matching efficiently.
g.text: Algorithm Comparison Summary
Naive: O(n×m) | KMP: O(n+m) | Boyer-Moore: O(n×m) worst, O(n/m) avg | Rabin-Karp: O(n+m) avg
Finite Automata: O(n) search | Space: Naive O(1), KMP O(m), Boyer-Moore O(m+σ), FA O(m×σ)
Exam Tip: Practice prefix function computation - it's a common 10-mark question in exams.
Choose KMP for predictable performance, Boyer-Moore for text with large alphabet characters.
